replies.text:
Established industry figures may start to expire. If you think about Robert Downey Junior, he had money in the bank. Getting him to sign onto Marvel long term would have made him sickeningly rich. But I think Brie Larson was probably on less than 10 million per picture (despite being an academy award winner). Amandla Stenberg, Rachel Zegler, were put in primo roles, but they were being manufactured. For all of the press coverage, it's possible they can barely afford their apartments. Hollywood threw Chloe Zhao, Nia DaCosta, Leslie Headland and Cate Shortland prime gigs which were incongruous with their earlier work. Cutting someone adrift who has never had a hit would be pretty easy.

If Hollywood pauses their DEI efforts, then they may not have talent on deck. If you recall the story of the Disney animators, it was that the people who had been doing it since the 90s were all put out to pasture. Similarly more broadly, people seemed to install executives and directors as diversity work experience programs. Getting the ideological hires out (who think talent is a patriarchal construct) might be complex. Having messed with the pipeline talent could mean that Hollywood struggles for a couple years. Talent on the ground is slight. Most directors you know would probably be in the 55-80 age bracket. And unearthing new talent is something Kathleen Kennedy or HR can't do.

If the talent is hollowed out, that can be the rationale to try again. They DO need to unearth new talent. If they can't spot talent, then they may revert to the old safe Hollywood template, have it fail because talent is short, and then immediately revert.

replies.author: @LeeHawkinsPhoto
replies.text: I would say that it’s fine for entertainers to voice their personal opinions on things, but that it comes at their own peril. They may ingratiate themselves to some people, but they will certainly m alienate others. If you want everyone to like you, it’s probably smarter to stay quiet…especially if you want work and you want the masses and to appreciate your work enough to keep buying it. Hollywood types walk a minefield though—they have come to expect each other to pledge allegiance to the cause du jour to get work—while the elite causes are no longer in line with what audiences care about. So while I get that they’re in a tough spot, audiences by and large are the real employers in mass media…so I think it’s best not to alienate audiences. Actors are especially ought to be careful—they may get to thinking that their brilliance on screen is all their own and that it transfers to real life—when the reality is that they are just great at owning someone else’s carefully thought out written words. Maybe it’s best if you just stay in character and on script and otherwise just express appreciation for your fans and the people who make you look good, so you can stay admirable and employable.
